title: Equations and Inequalities Problem Solving
why: To solve word problems with equations and inequalities, you pull the math out of the words — using fact families, missing-number thinking, and phrases like "at least" and "more than." Warm up all of these and the word problems become a plan.
skills: Reading at least / at most / more than
Turning words into a number sentence

diagnostic.q: "3 more than a number is 8." Which equation?

learn.heading: Words to math
learn.intro: Word problems hide a number sentence inside the words. Find the unknown, pick the operation, and write an equation (=) or an inequality (< or >). Then solve and check.
examples: "4 more than a number is 10" → n + 4 = 10 → n = 6 (since 10 − 4 = 6).
Phrase clues: "more than" → >, "less than" → <, "at least" → that number or bigger.

tiers.intro: One small step at a time.

items.q: A. A number plus 2 is 7. What is the number?
B. 3 groups of a number make 12. What is the number?

items.hint: "More than" means bigger, which is the > symbol.

tiers.intro: Match the story to the math, then solve.

items.q: A. "Maya had some stickers and got 5 more, now she has 12." Which equation?
items.hint: She started with s, added 5, and ended at 12: s + 5 = 12.

items.q: B. Solve that one: s + 5 = 12. How many did she start with?
A. 4 friends split some marbles equally and each got 3. How many marbles in all?
B. "You need at least 8 points to win." Which inequality (points = p)?
items.hint: "At least 8" means 8 or more, so p is 8 or greater.

exit.q: 1. A number plus 6 is 14. What is the number?
2. "The bag holds more than 10 apples." Which inequality (apples = a)?
